texts.orig: Fossil fuels have been a mainstay of the energy sector for well over a century and this trend is likely to continue for many more years with coal, gas and oil inventories continually being discovered and exploited for use as fuel for energy production globally [1].

texts.orig: Of particular concern is the greenhouse gases associated with both the generation and combustion of fossil fuels. The increase in atmospheric carbon dioxide (CO2) has been seen to closely correlate with increases in average global temperatures [2,3]. Along with similar effects associated with the anthropogenic release of sulfur oxides (SO x ) and nitrogen oxides (NO x ), global warming will continue to be a systemic problem connected with the world's hunger for sources of economic and reliable energy.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane stripping of carbon dioxide from an aqueous 30% (w/w) potassium carbonate solvent was achieved using two types of polymeric flat sheet microporous membranes; a polytetrofluoroethylene (PTFE) membrane and an asymmetric polyether sulfone membrane with a proprietary hydrophobic surface coating. Both membranes were tested at stripping temperatures of 60-100 ◦ C. The asymmetric membrane performed well at all temperatures. However, the mass transfer performance of the PTFE membrane was not sustained at higher temperatures, probably as a result of pore wetting by solvent intrusion. Calculations suggested that up to 72% of the membrane was wetted. In industrial applications, the use of higher temperatures could also lead to condensation of water vapour in the membrane support structure.
texts.text: Membrane stripping of carbon dioxide from an aqueous 30% (w/w) potassium carbonate solvent was achieved using two types of polymeric flat sheet microporous membranes; a polytetrofluoroethylene (PTFE) membrane and an asymmetric polyether sulfone membrane with a proprietary hydrophobic surface coating. Both membranes were tested at stripping temperatures of 60-100 ◦ C. The asymmetric membrane performed well at all temperatures. However, the mass transfer performance of the PTFE membrane was not sustained at higher temperatures, probably as a result of pore wetting by solvent intrusion. Calculations suggested that up to 72% of the membrane was wetted. In industrial applications, the use of higher temperatures could also lead to condensation of water vapour in the membrane support structure.

texts.orig: gas [8]. This interface permits mass transfer without direct fluid mixing therefore the solvent flow can be controlled independently of the gas. Because of this, membrane contactors do not suffer from the effects of entrainment, channelling, flooding and foaming observed in a traditional column. They can be operated in any orientation, whereas columns must be operated vertically. The mass transfer area is well defined and easily predicted as it is directly proportional to the surface area of the membrane. Most importantly, membrane contactors are compact, providing greater mass transfer contact area per unit volume than can be provided by conventional columns. As shown in Table 1, the mass transfer area per unit volume is estimated to be between 4 and 30 times greater in a membrane contactor than in a traditional column. Fig. 1 shows a probable layout for membrane absorption and desorption. In this case multiple membrane modules would replace both the packed column absorber and stripper.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A vast amount of research in this field has focused on absorption membrane contactors [9-16]. This work has shown that the extent of membrane pore wetting is crucial to system performance. That is, if the membrane pores remain gas-filled, mass transfer coefficients are comparable to those in a column contactors [7]. However, if the membrane pores fill with solvent, mass transfer coefficients fall significantly, to an extent where they are less competitive than the traditional column arrangement. The main criteria for membrane selection to minimize such pore wetting are the pore size, contact angle (a measure of hydrophobicity), solvent surface tension, chemical resistance to the solvent [4,13] and temperature. Correct selection of these parameters prevents pore wetting that acts to decrease the mass transfer of the system

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The study by Kosaraju et al. [19] considered both membrane gas absorption and stripping. The issue of pore wetting was suc- cessfully avoided through the use of porous membranes having an ultrathin dense skin. However, this approach led to a significantly higher membrane resistance than for the polypropylene alternative and it was not possible to completely eliminate leakage of solvent molecules through the membrane skin. Further, these experiments appeared to have only been conducted at ambient temperature. Koonaphapdeelert et al. used ceramic membranes [20] for membrane stripping which have the advantage of temperature stability, but cannot provide the same mass transfer area per unit volume of polymeric systems.

texts.orig: Fig. 1. This diagram illustrates a likely arrangement for the proposed membrane absorber/desorber system. The flue gas is sent to multiple membrane gas absorbers (MGA) operating at low temperature where CO2 is absorbed into the solvent and the remaining treated flue gas is released to the atmosphere. The rich solvent is pumped to multiple MGA units operating at higher temperatures where the CO2 is stripped back from the solvent and sent to storage.

texts.orig: The above model is based on pore wetting occurring through solvent intrusion, which is the usual mechanism in gas absorption processes. However, some researchers [33-35] have also described pore wetting associated with re-condensation of evaporated water within the membrane pore structure. These droplets may then partially or completely block the membrane pores increasing the overall resistance of the system.

However, some researchers [33-35] have also described pore wetting associated with re-condensation of evaporated water within the membrane pore structure. These droplets may then partially or completely block the membrane pores increasing the overall resistance of the system.

texts.orig: The flat sheet membrane holder was manufactured in-house from stainless steel (Fig. 6). The upper liquid chamber contained a magnetically driven stirrer that enabled controlled turbulence of the liquid layer immediately above the membrane. The stirrer was driven via a pulley drive connected to an electronic stirrer motor controller (Heidolph Instruments, Model RZR 2020, Germany). This enabled the stirrer to be controlled between 40 and 2000 rpm. A three blade propeller-type impeller of diameter 2.1 cm mixed the liquid phase while baffles that were 1/10 the diameter of the chamber (0.41 cm) ensured that vortexes were eliminated. The baffles were held in place by machined Teflon braces and stainless steel circlips (not show in diagram).

texts.orig: Surface images of the membranes were taken using a Philips XL30 FEG Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FEI Company, USA). Membranes were prepared by placing them onto aluminium SEM stage stubs (ProSciTech, Standard pin type SEM mounts -G040, Australia) using carbonized conducting adhesive SEM tabs (ProSciTech, IA022, Australia). They were then gold coated using a plasma reactor (Dynavac, SC100M, Australia) generating an argon (BOC Gases, 99.99% purity, Australia) plasma at sub atmospheric pressure. Imaging was undertaken in low vacuum mode (0.63 mBar) to reduce photodegradation of the membrane surface [41]. The working distance was optimized to approximately 11 mm such that the images had sufficient depth of field without affecting image resolution. Voltage (5-15 kV) and spot size was adjusted to minimize membrane degradation.

texts.orig: The use of stirring on the liquid side of the membrane minimizes the boundary layer in this phase (Fig. 2). Although it is not practical to use stirring in an industrial setting, an understanding of how turbulence in this layer affects the overall mass transfer is of great importance [36]. As illustrated, a stirrer speed of 0 rpm results in a flux that is considerably lower then that of the highly agitated cases for all temperatures observed. Under such conditions, the resistance of the boundary layer is high, implying that the HCO -3 concentration ( C B in Fig. 2) is low at the membrane surface. Correspondingly the available carbon dioxide ( C A in Fig. 2) is unable to form through conversion of the bicarbonate and hence flux is reduced. At higher stirrer speeds (>100 rpm), the flux is independent of stirrer speed, validating the assumption that mass transfer is in the fast regime and hence independent of the liquid phase mass transfer coefficient.

texts.orig: The increase in temperature of the aqueous potassium carbonate not only increases the carbon dioxide vapour pressure but also that of the water solvent [44]. This increases the driving force for migration of water through the membrane pore and thus the potential for water re-condensation within the pore structure. While such water re-condensation would be unlikely in the present experiments, due to the use of a dry sweep gas and a small membrane area, this may become an issue in larger scale work, where the sweep gas can be water saturated.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While PTFE is the standard material used in membrane gas absorption applications, it is very expensive. To this end this study considered a surface-modified polysulfone (PALL Supor 200 © R) membrane as a comparison to the PTFE membrane. This membrane has a comparable hydrophobicity and breakthrough pressure (Table 2), but is cheaper, due to the use of the cheaper polysulfone material for the bulk of the membrane.

texts.orig: The PALL membrane is also asymmetric; comprising a microporous active layer bonded to a coarsely woven polysulfone support (Fig. 4). In an industrial application, such a microporous support will also mean that any water-recondensation is less likely to block the flow of carbon dioxide. Conversely, in the PTFE membrane the pores are uniform in size throughout the cross section of the membrane. This means that they can be blocked more readily by condensing water vapour.

texts.orig: As a comparison, the extent of wetting by water re-condensation that would be required to provide the same membrane resistance was also calculated. The distinguishing feature between these two approaches is that for solvent breakthrough (Eq. (9)), mass transfer through the wetted area is enhanced by reaction, whereas for water condensation (Eq. (10)), no such enhancement occurs. It is readily apparent that only a very small extent of wetting due to water condensation can have a dramatic effect, with the wetting fractions here representing less than 2% of the membrane depth (Fig. 12b). Thus, in any industrial applications of this technology the use of composite membrane support structures and careful module design would be necessary to ensure that pore blockage by this phenomenon does not occur.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusions
texts.text: 5. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane stripping of carbon dioxide using polymeric alternatives has been successfully demonstrated. It has been shown that polymeric membranes can be successfully used for this purpose up to operating temperatures of 100 ◦ C, with overall mass transfer coefficients in the range of 1-2.5 × 10 -4 m/s. However, as temperature increases, membrane wetting becomes an increasing problem.
texts.text: Membrane stripping of carbon dioxide using polymeric alternatives has been successfully demonstrated. It has been shown that polymeric membranes can be successfully used for this purpose up to operating temperatures of 100 ◦ C, with overall mass transfer coefficients in the range of 1-2.5 × 10 -4 m/s. However, as temperature increases, membrane wetting becomes an increasing problem.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the present work, this membrane wetting probably arose from intrusion of the liquid solvent into the membrane pores, with calculations suggesting that up to 72% of the membrane was wetted. This effect is exacerbated as temperature increases due to reducing viscosity and surface tension. In industrial applications, the use of higher temperatures could also lead to condensation of water vapour in the membrane support structure. The impact of small amounts of condensed water has a major impact, as this aqueous phase does not facilitate CO2 transport to the same extent as the potassium carbonate solvent.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A polymeric alternative to PTFE was analyzed and shown to have superior mass transfer. As a composite membrane manufactured with a hydrophobic surface but otherwise made substantially from relatively inexpensive polysulfone, such a membrane would reduce the capital costs involved in the scale up of this technology.
